intro: Moving from C# to TypeScript transitions a backend developer into a modern, static-typed environment engineered to scale JavaScript development across the web tier. TypeScript perfectly bridges the conceptual gap for C# developers by providing familiar structural interfaces, generic parameters, class abstractions, and structural type checkers. The entire type system compile-time layer compiles down to standard JavaScript, providing frictionless execution across any JS host environment.

diffs.from: Static, nominal typing layout where type equivalence is strictly bound to explicit name definitions and declarations.
diffs.to: Static, structural typing layout where type equivalence is determined entirely by the shapes and properties of the object structures.

diffs.from: Verbose, class-centric structure requiring access scope declarations, assembly imports, and strict namespaces.
diffs.to: Modern, expressive layout supporting type unions, literal types, intersection states, and structural interfaces without runtime metadata.

diffs.from: Rigid memory layout optimizing value types on stack and class objects across generational garbage-collected segments.
diffs.to: Abstracted runtime memory mapping dictated by the underlying JavaScript host engine's automatic garbage collection tools.

diffs.from: Multi-threaded scheduling via Tasks, using advanced thread pool tracking loops and synchronized critical block sections.
diffs.to: Single-threaded event loop engine routing async operations via native Promises and non-blocking macro/micro task queues.

diffs.from: Deeply integrated .NET BCL framework providing multi-threaded structures, security foundations, and data abstractions.
diffs.to: Standard JavaScript runtime primitives combined with comprehensive type definitions covering DOM or Node.js API suites.

examples.title: Interface and Object Definition
examples.description: Compares a standard nominal C# interface implementation with structural type verification inside TypeScript.

examples.title: Union Types vs Polymorphism
examples.description: Contrasts C# subclass hierarchy structures against an idiomatic TypeScript structural union type matching pattern.

tips.title: Understand Structural Typing
tips.body: C# is nominal: two identical classes with different names cannot be interchanged. TypeScript is structural: if two objects share the exact same properties, they are considered structural equivalents regardless of name.

tips.title: Leverage Advanced Union Types
tips.body: Avoid deep inheritance trees for data models. Take advantage of TypeScript's union types (`string | number`) and discriminated unions to handle conditional model varieties concisely.

tips.title: Activate Strict Compiler Flags
tips.body: Ensure maximum application safety by turning on `strict: true` inside the `tsconfig.json` compiler options file. This activates strict null checking to replicate C#'s null-safety safeguards.

tips.title: Types Vanish at Runtime
tips.body: Remember that TypeScript undergoes type erasure. Interfaces and type declarations disappear entirely after compilation; you cannot use C#-style reflection (`typeof` or `is`) to inspect complex types at runtime.
